food_subgroup: Cooked fish dish in spiced curry sauce

ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked, in sauce or gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for North Indian fish curry; A0CKP (fish in sauce/gravy) is the closest defensible analogous category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for fish curry as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: cooked in homemade golden curry sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of North Indian fish curry is approximately 300 g, comprising boneless fish pieces in a generous portion of spiced golden curry sauce, usually served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Fish curry, prepared dish — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), Table of Cooked Dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports fish curry as a cooked Indian dish per 100 g edible portion. Values adapted to represent a mildly spiced North Indian style with boneless white fish, onion-tomato-based golden sauce, and moderate oil. The description 'mildly spiced golden homemade sauce' is consistent with a turmeric-dominant, low-chilli preparation. Sodium estimated from typical restaurant preparation; ICMR-NIN baseline adjusted upward by approximately 80 mg/100 g to reflect restaurant-level salting consistent with Singapore North Indian restaurant norms.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish curry dish — analogous estimate based on fish-based curry with low-GI spiced sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for North Indian fish curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is estimated from the analogous category of fish-based curry dishes with onion-tomato sauce and minimal starchy carbohydrate content. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are onion and tomato (both low-GI foods, GI ~15–35). Fish itself contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. The estimated GI of 38 is consistent with published values for similar low-carbohydrate fish-based curry preparations. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 11.4) / 100 = 4.3, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 3.8 × 3 = 11.4 g.

hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 38 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, fish curry has a low glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; pairing with cauliflower rice or a small portion of basmati rice is preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein, moderate unsaturated fat, and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish curry provides lean protein and a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (approximately 4.3:1.4 g per 100 g), and the turmeric and cumin in the sauce have documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, sodium content warrants monitoring in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Curry (North Indian Style) is a cooked seafood dish consisting of boneless white fish fillets simmered in a mildly spiced, golden-coloured homemade curry sauce. The sauce is typically built on a base of sautéed onion, tomato, ginger, and garlic, seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and minimal chilli, giving it a warm golden hue and a mild, aromatic flavour profile rather than a fiery heat. The fish is tender and flakes easily, absorbing the fragrant sauce during cooking. This preparation is characteristic of North Indian restaurant cuisine as served in Singapore, where it is offered at establishments such as Curry Village, Bawarchi, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, and Aryana. Nutritionally, fish curry is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 118 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and only 3.8 g carbohydrate, making it one of the more nutritionally favourable curry options. The glycemic index is estimated at 38 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 300 g restaurant serving is approximately 4.3, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations (approximately 420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible. It is commonly served with basmati rice, naan, or roti, and is a staple of North Indian restaurant menus across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish curry?
Is fish curry good for diabetics?
Is fish curry high in sodium?
What is the GI of fish curry?
What fish is used in North Indian fish curry?
How much protein is in fish curry?
Can I eat fish curry if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fish curry data for cooked Indian dishes, which is the highest-priority approved source for Indian cuisine. The adaptation accounts for the mildly spiced, golden-sauce North Indian restaurant style described in the input. No exact match for this specific restaurant preparation exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework and peer-reviewed international GI tables, as no direct measurement for North Indian fish curry is published.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN fish curry entry and is highly variable depending on fish species used. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in plant-oil-based Indian curry preparations per ICMR-NIN data. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects an upward adjustment from the ICMR-NIN baseline to account for restaurant-level salting typical of Singapore North Indian restaurants. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu label 'Fish Curry (Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical form 'Fish Curry (North Indian Style)' to remove menu-style clutter while preserving cuisine specificity. The cuisine field has been corrected from the restaurant name to the cuisine category 'North Indian'. Price data from the input (SGD 17.90–20.90) is not carried into the RESULT template as it is a delivery-platform commercial attribute outside the nutritional scope of this record.
